texto: Here is the life you have tried to throw away. Here is your second chance. Here is the destiny you have tried to shake off by inventing a hundred false roles, a hundred false identities for yourself. It will look at first like disaster, but is really good fortune in disguise, since fate too knows how to follow your evasions through a hundred forms of its own. Now you will become at last the one you intended to be.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a David Malouf: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de sentido da existência e experiência vivida. O trecho “Here is the life you have tried to throw away. Here is your second chance. Here is the destiny yo…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que vida, identity, mask pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de termino e vida.

texto: Fiction, with its preference for what is small and might elsewhere seem irrelevant; its facility for smuggling us into another skin and allowing us to live a new life there; its painstaking devotion to what without it might go unnoticed and unseen; its respect for contingency, and the unlikely and odd; its willingness to expose itself to moments of low, almost animal being and make them nobly illuminating, can deliver truths we might not otherwise stumble on.
explicacao: Citação de David Malouf sobre passagem do tempo e prioridades. “Fiction, with its preference for what is small and might elsewhere seem irrelevant; its facility …” articula reflexao, essays, fiction em registro de reflexao, com relevância para contextos como fiction e literature.

texto: What drew him back was something altogether more personal, to a history where, in the pain and longing of adolescence, he was still standing on the corner of Queen and Albert Streets waiting for someone that he knew would never appear. He had long understood that one of his selves, the earliest and most vulnerable, had never left this place, and this original and clearest view of things could be recovered only through what had first come to him in the glow of its ordinary light and weather...it was the light they appeared in that was the point, and that at least had not changed.
explicacao: Citação de David Malouf sobre limites e escolhas conscientes. “What drew him back was something altogether more personal, to a history where, in the pain and lo…” articula familia, adolescence, history em registro de familia, com relevância para contextos como mudanca e history.

texto: So long as we are driven by the need to make up for our needs; by the restless sense that we are not yet fully assured of our place in the world and our hold on its swarming phenomena; so long as there is more to be discovered and made, more to grasp for and make real, we must go on inventing ourselves.

texto: What else should our lives be but a continual series of beginnings, of painful settings out into the unknown, pushing off from the edges of consciousness into the mystery of what we have not yet become.

texto: The planet, saved for another day, stokes upits slow-burning gases and toxic dust, gold rift and scarletgash that take our breath away; a world at its interminableshow of holy dying. And we go with it, the oldgatherer and hunter. To its gaudy-day, though the contributionis small, adding our handsel of warm clay.

texto: And if other old men must be willing, at the end, to push up off their deathbed and adventure out into the unknown, how much more willing must that man be whose whole life has been just such a daily exercise of adventuring, even in the stillness of his own garden? I mean, the poet.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de David Malouf que provoca sentido da existência e experiência vivida. Ao questionar “And if other old men must be willing, at the end, to push up off their deathbed and adventure out…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre reflexao, poet, willing, com foco em luto e termino.

texto: So these things happen, deep in our lives. We do not speak of them. We hide them even from ourselves, but they do not leave us.

texto: But here we call it Spring, when a young man’s fancy turns,fitfully, lightly, to idling in the sun,to touching in the dark. And the old man's?To worms in their garden box; stepping asidea moment in a poem that will remember,fitfully, who made it and the discordand stammer, and change of heart and catch of breathit sprang from. A bending downlightly to touch the earth.
